SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper includes a broad set of experiments and evaluations supporting its claims: phonetic- and character-level models trained on multiple corpora, multiple quantitative metrics (syllabic misclassifications, per-word reconstruction accuracy, trigram similarity, coverage) and a large-scale human evaluation (54% of machine poems judged human; a machine poem rated best). Related work is reported as thorough and the authors discuss failure modes and limitations (transliteration/pronunciation dictionary errors, OOV impacts, and future morpheme-based approaches). However, the summary does not mention any ablation or sensitivity analyses and code is not provided, so reproducibility and understanding of component contributions have minor gaps. | B(4): The paper presents a fairly comprehensive experimental suite (phonetic-level and character-level models; multiple intrinsic metrics such as syllabic errors, word reconstruction accuracy, n-gram similarity and coverage; and a large-scale extrinsic human evaluation with 54% judged as human) and compares against reasonable baselines. Related work coverage is reported as thorough and several failure modes and limitations (transliteration and pronunciation-dictionary OOVs, future morpheme-based work) are explicitly discussed. However, the summary does not mention ablation studies or sensitivity analyses of key design choices, and while hyperparameters are reported reproducibility is weakened by lack of code and the complex transliteration pipeline. These constitute minor but important gaps, so I score the paper a 4.

Related work is reported as thorough and the authors discuss failure modes and limitations (transliteration/pronunciation dictionary errors, OOV impacts, and future morpheme-based approaches). However, the summary does not mention any ablation or sensitivity analyses and code is not provided, so reproducibility and understanding of component contributions have minor gaps.

Related work coverage is reported as thorough and several failure modes and limitations (transliteration and pronunciation-dictionary OOVs, future morpheme-based work) are explicitly discussed. These constitute minor but important gaps, so I score the paper a 4.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper targets an important and challenging problem—automatic generation of rhythmic, formal poetry—by introducing two novel approaches (a phonetic-level LSTM and a constraint-based character model with a weighted finite-state machine) that directly model form and content. The large-scale extrinsic evaluation, where machine poems were judged human 54% of the time and one machine poem was rated best, indicates the methods can materially change expectations for generated verse and suggests practical utility. While transliteration and OOV issues limit immediate robustness, the phonetic representation and constraint-decoding paradigm open clear new directions (prosody-aware generation, constrained decoding for arbitrary forms) that other researchers and practitioners are likely to build on. | B(4): The paper tackles a meaningful problem—automatic generation of rhythmic, form-constrained poetry—and introduces two complementary methods (a phonetic-level neural LM that captures rhyme, rhythm and alliteration, and a character-level LM constrained via a discriminative weighted FSA) that advance controllable, stylistic text generation. Strong extrinsic evidence (participants judged machine-generated poems human-written 54% of the time and one machine poem rated best) suggests these approaches materially change practical expectations for machine-generated verse. While transliteration/pronunciation OOV issues and other limitations may limit immediate robustness, the techniques open clear new research directions (phonetic encodings, hybrid constraint mechanisms) and are likely to influence follow-on work in stylized and constrained generation beyond poetry.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper targets an important and challenging problem—automatic generation of rhythmic, formal poetry—by introducing two novel approaches (a phonetic-level LSTM and a constraint-based character model with a weighted finite-state machine) that directly model form and content. The large-scale extrinsic evaluation, where machine poems were judged human 54% of the time and one machine poem was rated best, indicates the methods can materially change expectations for generated verse and suggests practical utility. While transliteration and OOV issues limit immediate robustness, the phonetic representation and constraint-decoding paradigm open clear new directions (prosody-aware generation, constrained decoding for arbitrary forms) that other researchers and practitioners are likely to build on.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper tackles a meaningful problem—automatic generation of rhythmic, form-constrained poetry—and introduces two complementary methods (a phonetic-level neural LM that captures rhyme, rhythm and alliteration, and a character-level LM constrained via a discriminative weighted FSA) that advance controllable, stylistic text generation. Strong extrinsic evidence (participants judged machine-generated poems human-written 54% of the time and one machine poem rated best) suggests these approaches materially change practical expectations for machine-generated verse. While transliteration/pronunciation OOV issues and other limitations may limit immediate robustness, the techniques open clear new research directions (phonetic encodings, hybrid constraint mechanisms) and are likely to influence follow-on work in stylized and constrained generation beyond poetry.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper evaluates its methods against sensible baselines (an unconstrained character model and human-generated poetry) and uses multiple quantitative metrics (syllabic misclassifications, word reconstruction accuracy, per-line trigram similarity, coverage) plus a large-scale human judgment study (54% of machine poems judged as human). Comparisons appear fair in that shared datasets and evaluations are reported, and limitations (transliteration and OOV issues) are acknowledged. However, only one machine baseline is listed in the summary, so there may be minor omissions of other competitive algorithmic baselines despite the paper claiming thorough related-work coverage. | B(4): The paper compares its methods to reasonable baselines (an unconstrained character model and human-generated poetry) and evaluates them on shared datasets and multiple metrics (syllabic mistakes, word reconstruction accuracy, trigram similarity, and human judgments), and its related-work coverage is reported as thorough. Differences between the phonetic-level LM and the constraint-based character approach are described and analyzed via appropriate form-focused metrics. However, important modern baselines from other constrained or phonetic-aware poetry-generation systems are not mentioned among the explicit baselines, so comparisons are good but not fully comprehensive.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates its methods against sensible baselines (an unconstrained character model and human-generated poetry) and uses multiple quantitative metrics (syllabic misclassifications, word reconstruction accuracy, per-line trigram similarity, coverage) plus a large-scale human judgment study (54% of machine poems judged as human). Comparisons appear fair in that shared datasets and evaluations are reported, and limitations (transliteration and OOV issues) are acknowledged. However, only one machine baseline is listed in the summary, so there may be minor omissions of other competitive algorithmic baselines despite the paper claiming thorough related-work coverage.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares its methods to reasonable baselines (an unconstrained character model and human-generated poetry) and evaluates them on shared datasets and multiple metrics (syllabic mistakes, word reconstruction accuracy, trigram similarity, and human judgments), and its related-work coverage is reported as thorough. Differences between the phonetic-level LM and the constraint-based character approach are described and analyzed via appropriate form-focused metrics. However, important modern baselines from other constrained or phonetic-aware poetry-generation systems are not mentioned among the explicit baselines, so comparisons are good but not fully comprehensive.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper introduces two distinct methodological innovations: a phonetic-level LSTM trained on phonetic encodings (CMU dictionary, letter-to-sound rules, HMM-based transliteration) to capture rhyme/rhythm/alliteration, and a character-level generative LM constrained at generation time by a discriminative weighted finite-state machine to enforce arbitrary poetic forms. These approaches clearly differentiate the contribution from prior work by explicitly modeling phonetic form and by integrating constraint-based FSA enforcement with neural generation, which is a non-trivial combination beyond standard character/word LMs. The strong extrinsic evaluation results (54% of machine poems judged human; a machine poem rated best) make the contribution likely to inform or surprise experts, supporting notable novelty rather than merely incremental change. | B(4): The paper presents two distinct, novel approaches—a phonetic-level LSTM trained on phonetic encodings (CMU dictionary + letter-to-sound rules and HMM-based transliteration) and a character-level generative LM coupled with a discriminative weighted finite-state machine to enforce rhythmic/form constraints—which together offer a new way to model poetic form in neural generation. These techniques are clearly differentiated from prior work in the summary and go beyond minor extensions by combining phonetic modeling with constraint-based decoding for arbitrary forms; the reported large-scale human evaluation (54% judged as human) further indicates practical novelty.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper introduces two distinct methodological innovations: a phonetic-level LSTM trained on phonetic encodings (CMU dictionary, letter-to-sound rules, HMM-based transliteration) to capture rhyme/rhythm/alliteration, and a character-level generative LM constrained at generation time by a discriminative weighted finite-state machine to enforce arbitrary poetic forms. These approaches clearly differentiate the contribution from prior work by explicitly modeling phonetic form and by integrating constraint-based FSA enforcement with neural generation, which is a non-trivial combination beyond standard character/word LMs. The strong extrinsic evaluation results (54% of machine poems judged human; a machine poem rated best) make the contribution likely to inform or surprise experts, supporting notable novelty rather than merely incremental change.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents two distinct, novel approaches—a phonetic-level LSTM trained on phonetic encodings (CMU dictionary + letter-to-sound rules and HMM-based transliteration) and a character-level generative LM coupled with a discriminative weighted finite-state machine to enforce rhythmic/form constraints—which together offer a new way to model poetic form in neural generation. These techniques are clearly differentiated from prior work in the summary and go beyond minor extensions by combining phonetic modeling with constraint-based decoding for arbitrary forms; the reported large-scale human evaluation (54% judged as human) further indicates practical novelty.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper appears well-organized—methods (phonetic-level and character-level approaches), datasets, baselines, evaluation metrics, and limitations are all presented in sequence, suggesting a clear intro→methods→experiments→conclusion flow. The main contributions are stated early (two novel methodologies) and related work coverage is described as thorough; reporting of hyperparameters and many evaluation metrics supports clear exposition. The summary does not explicitly mention notation or the quality of figures/tables, but the presence of detailed metrics and reported limitations implies generally consistent presentation; overall writing is described as "adequate," so only minor presentation issues are likely. | B(4): The paper is well organized with a clear flow from problem and two proposed methods (phonetic-level LSTM and constrained character-level model) through experiments on multiple datasets and human evaluation. The main contributions are stated early, related work coverage is thorough, and technical details (including hyperparameters) are reported, indicating notation and methods are presented adequately. Figures/tables and examples appear informative given the evaluation metrics and human-judgment results, though writing is only adequate overall and discussions of transliteration/pronunciation limitations could be clarified further.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper appears well-organized—methods (phonetic-level and character-level approaches), datasets, baselines, evaluation metrics, and limitations are all presented in sequence, suggesting a clear intro→methods→experiments→conclusion flow. The main contributions are stated early (two novel methodologies) and related work coverage is described as thorough; reporting of hyperparameters and many evaluation metrics supports clear exposition. The summary does not explicitly mention notation or the quality of figures/tables, but the presence of detailed metrics and reported limitations implies generally consistent presentation; overall writing is described as "adequate," so only minor presentation issues are likely.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well organized with a clear flow from problem and two proposed methods (phonetic-level LSTM and constrained character-level model) through experiments on multiple datasets and human evaluation. The main contributions are stated early, related work coverage is thorough, and technical details (including hyperparameters) are reported, indicating notation and methods are presented adequately. Figures/tables and examples appear informative given the evaluation metrics and human-judgment results, though writing is only adequate overall and discussions of transliteration/pronunciation limitations could be clarified further.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper addresses automatic poetry generation using neural language models and constraint-based generation, which squarely falls within NLP research on structured text generation and is relevant to top-tier NLP/ML audiences. The methodology (phonetic-level LSTM, character-level LM with a discriminative WFSM) and the evaluation (intrinsic metrics plus large-scale human judgments) match standards expected at these venues. It is positioned with thorough related work and appropriate datasets, though the creative/genre-specific focus on poetry may narrow its audience somewhat, so it is a very good but not perfect fit. | B(5): This paper addresses automatic poetry generation using neural language models and formal constraint methods (phonetic-level LSTM, character-level LM with a discriminative WFSM), which squarely falls within NLP and ML research on generative modeling and structured sequence constraints. The methodology and evaluations (quantitative metrics, human judgments with a reported 54% human rating, datasets like Sonnets and Wikipedia, and hyperparameters reported) match the standards expected at top venues, and the authors provide thorough related-work coverage and clear limitations, indicating correct positioning for the NLP/ML community.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses automatic poetry generation using neural language models and constraint-based generation, which squarely falls within NLP research on structured text generation and is relevant to top-tier NLP/ML audiences. The methodology (phonetic-level LSTM, character-level LM with a discriminative WFSM) and the evaluation (intrinsic metrics plus large-scale human judgments) match standards expected at these venues. It is positioned with thorough related work and appropriate datasets, though the creative/genre-specific focus on poetry may narrow its audience somewhat, so it is a very good but not perfect fit.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses automatic poetry generation using neural language models and formal constraint methods (phonetic-level LSTM, character-level LM with a discriminative WFSM), which squarely falls within NLP and ML research on generative modeling and structured sequence constraints. The methodology and evaluations (quantitative metrics, human judgments with a reported 54% human rating, datasets like Sonnets and Wikipedia, and hyperparameters reported) match the standards expected at top venues, and the authors provide thorough related-work coverage and clear limitations, indicating correct positioning for the NLP/ML community.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper’s central claims are supported by empirical evidence: a phonetic-level LSTM and a character-level LM + discriminative WFSM are implemented and evaluated with objective metrics (syllabic misclassifications, word reconstruction, trigram similarity, coverage) and a large-scale human evaluation where machine poems were judged human 54% of the time and one machine poem rated best. Mathematical content appears minimal or nonessential and no incorrect derivations are reported. However, there are technical weaknesses that temper the claims—notably substantial transliteration/pronunciation-dictionary errors and low per-word accuracy on the Wikipedia corpus that limit the phonetic model’s generality, and the summary does not report statistical significance testing or full details of the human-evaluation controls (possible selection bias via Dale–Chall filtering). These are important but minor issues that do not invalidate the main experimental conclusions. | B(4): The paper states clear, testable claims and provides experimental evidence (phonetic LSTM, constrained character LM, multiple quantitative metrics and a large-scale human evaluation showing 54% of machine poems judged as human and one machine poem rated best). There are no complex mathematical derivations to verify, and the described experiments use reasonable baselines (unconstrained char model and human poetry) and multiple evaluation metrics. Minor concerns remain: the phonetic-model’s reliance on transliteration and pronunciation dictionaries (acknowledged by the authors) and the summary’s lack of explicit mention of statistical significance testing leave some uncertainty about the robustness of the evaluation. Overall, results and conclusions are supported by the presented evidence but with these caveats.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper’s central claims are supported by empirical evidence: a phonetic-level LSTM and a character-level LM + discriminative WFSM are implemented and evaluated with objective metrics (syllabic misclassifications, word reconstruction, trigram similarity, coverage) and a large-scale human evaluation where machine poems were judged human 54% of the time and one machine poem rated best. Mathematical content appears minimal or nonessential and no incorrect derivations are reported. However, there are technical weaknesses that temper the claims—notably substantial transliteration/pronunciation-dictionary errors and low per-word accuracy on the Wikipedia corpus that limit the phonetic model’s generality, and the summary does not report statistical significance testing or full details of the human-evaluation controls (possible selection bias via Dale–Chall filtering). These are important but minor issues that do not invalidate the main experimental conclusions.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper states clear, testable claims and provides experimental evidence (phonetic LSTM, constrained character LM, multiple quantitative metrics and a large-scale human evaluation showing 54% of machine poems judged as human and one machine poem rated best). There are no complex mathematical derivations to verify, and the described experiments use reasonable baselines (unconstrained char model and human poetry) and multiple evaluation metrics. Minor concerns remain: the phonetic-model’s reliance on transliteration and pronunciation dictionaries (acknowledged by the authors) and the summary’s lack of explicit mention of statistical significance testing leave some uncertainty about the robustness of the evaluation. Overall, results and conclusions are supported by the presented evidence but with these caveats.

main_claims: Propose two novel methodologies for automatic generation of rhythmic poetry: a phonetic-level neural language model and a constraint-based character-level approach.
A phonetic-level neural language model trained on phonetic encodings can learn implicit representations of both poetic form and content, capturing devices such as rhyme, rhythm and alliteration.

A character-level generative neural language model combined with a discriminative weighted finite-state machine can impose form constraints, allowing generation of coherent poetry with arbitrary forms and themes.
A large-scale extrinsic evaluation found participants judged machine-generated poems as human-written 54% of the time, and a machine-generated poem was rated best among evaluated poems.
_summary.methodology: Train an LSTM neural language model on phonetic encodings (using CMU pronunciation dictionary, letter-to-sound rules, and transliteration via an HMM + n-gram model) to learn form and content; alternatively, use a character-level generative neural LM for content and constrain generation with a discriminative weighted finite-state machine to enforce rhythmic/form constraints.
metrics: syllabic misclassifications (number of syllabic mistakes per line)
human judgment (% of machine poems judged as human)
Dale–Chall readability formula (for selecting human poems)
limitations: A significant number of errors in the phonetic-level model are likely caused by transliteration mistakes.
Pronunciation dictionaries suffer limitations for constructing syllabic stress classifiers (e.g., out-of-vocabulary words reduce accuracy).
Relatively low per-word accuracy on Wikipedia corpus due to high frequency of out-of-vocabulary words.
Authors note investigating morpheme-based models in future work as current character-based approaches may perform worse on complex and rare words.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - automatic poetry generation / neural language models
